narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction draws from your academic record, like being in the 4th decile for Year 1 grades and 3rd decile for Year 3—both below the typical student's 6th or 5th decile positions—along with your LSAT score of 31, which is notably lower than the average of about 37. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.7 stands out as stronger than the usual 3.2, and your upper-middle family income bracket is solid compared to peers. You're also in the majority as a full-time White female applicant, which aligns closely with most students in our dataset.

The model's decision was shaped most by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, likely because it signals potential challenges in sustaining performance under pressure. Right behind that was your LSAT score, pushing the prediction further in the same direction since it's well below what successful bar takers typically show. Your Year 1 decile came next in influence, again nudging toward failure relative to higher-achieving classmates. Family income played a moderate role in the same way, followed by your UGPA—which actually tried to counter the failure prediction but wasn't enough—then race, gender, and full-time status, each with slighter impacts all leaning toward risk.

Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower deciles and LSAT stand out as bigger hurdles, while your GPA gives you an edge many lack. To boost your future odds, focus on actionable steps like targeted test prep to lift metrics like LSAT if you retake it, or building stronger study habits to improve decile rankings in any ongoing academics. Seeking bar-specific tutoring or practice exams could address the patterns in your record that concerned the model most. With your solid GPA as a foundation, these changes could realistically shift your predicted path toward passing. You're already ahead in some areas—keep building on that resilience.
